name: Voynich Manuscript
description: The Voynich Manuscript is an illustrated codex hand-written in an unknown writing system. The vellum on which it is written has been carbon-dated to the early 15th century (1404-1438), and it may have been composed in Northern Italy during the Italian Renaissance. The manuscript is named after Wilfrid Voynich, a Polish book dealer who purchased it in 1912. The manuscript's text has defied all attempts at decipherment, leading to numerous theories about its origin, purpose, and meaning. These range from elaborate hoaxes and meaningless gibberish to coded alchemical or herbal treatises, or even a lost language. The illustrations feature strange plants, astronomical diagrams, and nude figures bathing in green liquids, further fueling speculation. Mainstream scholarship generally leans towards the manuscript being either a sophisticated hoax, a text encoded using a complex cipher, or a document written in a constructed language. The lack of any demonstrable meaning after decades of analysis suggests a deliberate attempt at obfuscation. However, proponents of alternative theories argue that the manuscript contains genuine information, perhaps pertaining to lost knowledge or a forgotten civilization. They point to the detailed illustrations and the consistent structure of the text as evidence against a simple hoax. The manuscript remains one of the most enduring mysteries in the world of cryptography and historical linguistics, continually prompting new research and debate.
subtitle: An Undeciphered Enigma of Ink and Vellum

claims.claim: The manuscript is a sophisticated hoax designed to deceive.
claims.evidence: The lack of decipherment despite extensive efforts; the unusual and seemingly nonsensical content.
claims.criticism: The complexity of the illustrations and text suggests a significant investment of time and resources, making a simple hoax less plausible. The consistent structure of the text suggests a deliberate system, not random gibberish.

claims.claim: The manuscript contains encoded information of significant value, possibly alchemical or herbal knowledge.
claims.evidence: The presence of illustrations resembling plants, astronomical objects, and anatomical diagrams.
claims.criticism: The 'plants' do not correspond to known species; the astronomical diagrams are often inaccurate or unusual; and the anatomical diagrams are unlike contemporary medical illustrations. No decipherment has yielded verifiable or useful information.

claims.claim: The manuscript is written in a lost or constructed language.
claims.evidence: The consistent linguistic structure of the text, suggesting a grammar and syntax.
claims.criticism: Despite numerous attempts, no connection to any known language (natural or constructed) has been established. The statistical properties of the text have been found to differ from those of known languages.

keyFigures.name: Gordon Rugg
keyFigures.role: Proposed a hoaxing method using Cardan grilles

keyFigures.name: Stephen Bax
keyFigures.role: Claimed to have deciphered some words using a semantic approach

mythologyConnections: Possible symbolic representation of ancient myths

timeline.event: Vellum is dated using radiocarbon dating
